title: Pork chops with mustard cream sauce
subtitle: Pan-fried pork with a Dijon and cream pan sauce
excerpt: Thick pork chops browned in butter and finished with a Dijon mustard and cream sauce made in the pan. A ten-minute dinner that works with both white wine and chicken stock as the liquid base.
sourceNotes: Pork with mustard and cream is a classic of the French brasserie tradition, widely adopted in British domestic cooking in the latter half of the twentieth century. The technique of building a cream sauce in the pork roasting pan draws on classic French sauce-making and appears in British cookbooks from the 1970s onwards.

content.text: The mustard cream sauce is made in the same pan as the chops, using the browned pan drippings as the flavour base. It takes 5 minutes once the pork has rested. The cream mellows the sharpness of the Dijon into something rounded and glossy; a little wholegrain mustard added at the end gives texture. Both types of mustard are better here than either alone.

Bone-in chops work best but boneless work fine. The key is thickness: a thin chop cooks too quickly for a proper crust to form and will be dry before the sauce is ready. Ask for chops at least 2.5 cm thick.

items.prepNote: about 250 g each, at least 2.5 cm thick

content.text: Season {{pork-chop}} pork chops well on both sides. Heat a frying pan over medium-high heat. Add the butter and, when foaming, lay in the chops. Cook for 4 minutes on the first side without moving, then turn and cook for 3 to 4 minutes more until cooked through. Remove and rest on a warm plate for 5 minutes.

In the same pan over medium heat, cook {{shallot}} finely chopped shallots (and garlic if using) for 2 minutes until soft. Add the thyme sprigs and pour in {{dry-white-wine}} of dry white wine or chicken stock. Let it bubble for 2 minutes, scraping up any browned bits from the base. Pour in {{double-cream}} of double cream and cook for 3 minutes until the sauce has thickened enough to coat the back of a spoon. Remove the thyme. Stir in {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard and the wholegrain mustard if using. Taste and season.
Return the chops to the pan and spoon the sauce over them. Scatter with parsley if using. Serve with egg noodles, mashed potato, or buttered greens.

items.symptom: Sauce is grainy or has split
items.cause: Heat was too high when the cream was added, or the mustard was added over excessive heat
items.fix: Keep the heat at medium when the cream goes in. If the sauce splits, remove from heat, add a splash of cold cream, and whisk. Add the mustard off the heat.

items.cause: Too much mustard or the wine was very acidic
items.fix: Add a pinch of sugar and an extra tablespoon of cream to balance.
A 2.5 cm pork chop needs 7 to 8 minutes total at medium-high heat. Pork is safe to eat with a slight blush of pink. Use a thermometer: 65°C at the thickest point is correct.

items.cause: Not enough reduction before the cream was added
items.fix: Reduce the wine by at least half before adding cream. The cream will thicken quickly once the liquid base is concentrated enough.

content.text: Pork with mustard and cream is one of those pan sauce techniques that crossed the Channel and settled comfortably into British domestic cooking. The French original, which uses Dijon mustard with crème fraîche in Burgundy and Normandy, is a restaurant and bistro dish in France; in Britain it became a quick weeknight dinner. Its popularity in the 1970s and 1980s, when British home cooks were enthusiastically adopting French pan sauce technique, gave it a permanent place in the domestic repertoire. The dish requires no special skill and rewards good-quality ingredients: a proper Dijon, real cream, and thick pork chops.
